video_title: Crypto Is Running Out of Reasons to Go Lower
gist: Bankless hosts David and Ryan debate whether crypto has bottomed using a four-cycle Bitcoin chart, cover cooling CPI against an escalating Iran war and oil-price dynamics, and dig into the Robin Hood chain vs. Base L2 rivalry (including Jesse Pollak's admission Base's creator-coin bet was wrong) and the resulting ETH tokenomics debate over whether Ethereum should optimize for fee revenue or pure store-of-value status.

insights.claim: A chart overlaying Bitcoin's four historical bear-to-bull cycles (2014, 2018, 2022, 2026) shows a consistent bear/pre-bull/first-bull/second-bull pattern; if it holds, the market is close to but has not yet hit bottom, with a final capitulation likely in the next two to three months followed by a sideways grind into 2027 and a stronger 2028.
insights.speaker: Ryan
insights.so_what: Watch for a possible final capitulation low around August-October 2026 under this pattern before positioning for the next bull leg.
insights.quote: Never fade the cycle.

insights.claim: A Brian Armstrong Twitter/X poll of roughly 31,000 votes on whether Bitcoin has bottomed came back nearly split, with 44% saying yes and 55% saying no.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: Retail sentiment on the bottom call is genuinely divided, so it shouldn't be treated as a decisive signal in either direction.
insights.quote: 44% said yes, the bottom is in. 55% said no, the bottom is not in.

insights.claim: A genuine capitulation bottom would likely require a broader equity shock, such as a 10-20% NASDAQ selloff that could pull Bitcoin down toward the high-$20,000s from its then-current level near $29,000, rather than the modest pullback occurring at time of recording.
insights.speaker: Ryan
insights.so_what: A NASDAQ drawdown of that magnitude is a leading trigger to watch for a true capitulation low, rather than crypto-specific weakness alone.
insights.quote: If we saw a 10 to 20% um NASDAQ selloff from here, things could still get uglier. I mean, that could be a final capitulation bottom.

insights.claim: Crypto commentator Good Alexander argued the market has run out of bearish catalysts because Bitcoin didn't break down despite the Iran war and Michael Saylor selling MSTR shares, Zcash didn't crash despite an AI-discovered privacy bug, and ETH rallied even after Vitalik Buterin disavowed gambling as a use case -- framing this as a sign of seller exhaustion.
insights.speaker: Good Alexander (X commentator, quoted)
insights.so_what: If negative catalysts stop moving price lower, it can signal seller exhaustion near a cycle bottom, though the hosts caution real downside risk still exists via a broader equity selloff.
insights.quote: Bitcoin can't go down with a war and Michael Sailor selling. Zcash can't go down with a day zero privacy bug discovered by AI. ETH books Robin Hood after Vitalic disavows gambling as a use case. The night is darkest before dawn. Bitches.

themes.theme: Macro Backdrop: Inflation and the Iran War

insights.claim: June US CPI inflation came in at 3.5% versus 3.8% expected, with core CPI at 2.6% versus 2.8% expected and the largest month-over-month inflation drop since May 2020, easing fears of near-term rate hikes that had spooked markets the prior month.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: Cooler-than-expected inflation removed a key overhang for risk assets and coincided with this week's crypto strength.
insights.quote: June CPI inflation falls to 3.5% below expectations of 3.8%.

insights.claim: Despite the cool CPI print, oil prices rose about 20% in July amid the intensifying Iran conflict, which the hosts flag as a lagging risk since higher energy costs typically feed into future CPI readings with a delay.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: The current disinflation trend could reverse if elevated energy prices persist, a risk worth monitoring before assuming the CPI relief is durable.

insights.claim: As of the fifth straight day of US strikes on Iran, the campaign is targeting Iranian command centers, air defense, missile/drone capabilities and coastal surveillance to force open the Strait of Hormuz; Trump has framed this as a new, second conflict distinct from the earlier one, resetting a fresh 60-day War Powers Act reporting window.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: An extended or escalating conflict raises tail risk for an oil-price shock that could spill into risk-asset sentiment, including crypto.

insights.claim: Despite intensifying conflict, oil at $78 remains the cheapest level of the entire Iran war period (which had ranged near $95 before falling to $66), because global buyers and US/Gulf producers have had time to reroute supply around the Strait of Hormuz, suggesting oil is unlikely to spike back to prior highs.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: Structural rerouting of global oil supply caps the upside energy-price risk to risk-asset narratives, supportive of continued risk-on positioning.
insights.quote: $78 oil is still the cheapest oil during this Iran war conflict in total.

insights.claim: Small domestic economic protests are re-emerging inside Iran as its economy decays under the reinstated naval blockade, suggesting Iran's negotiating leverage (mainly threatening the Strait of Hormuz) is eroding even as the conflict shows no clear end in sight, raising 'forever war' risk.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: A prolonged, open-ended conflict is a tail risk for energy markets and broader risk sentiment that investors should keep on the radar rather than assume resolves quickly.

themes.theme: Crypto Treasury Companies: Saylor and Tom Lee

insights.claim: Michael Saylor's Strategy (MSTR) sold zero Bitcoin this week -- a reversal from selling several thousand BTC worth roughly $300-400 million the prior week -- and instead raised $466 million via at-the-market MSTR share issuance, bringing cash reserves to about $3 billion, enough for roughly 20 months of dividend coverage.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: A roughly 20-month cash runway would carry Strategy through the projected bear-market tail per the cycle framework, reducing near-term forced-Bitcoin-selling risk.
insights.quote: He sold zero Bitcoin this week.

insights.claim: Peter Schiff argued Michael Saylor 'needlessly destroyed shareholder value' by issuing MSTR shares at a discount to Bitcoin net asset value instead of selling Bitcoin itself, a critique the hosts say is actually a bear case for MSTR equity specifically, not for Bitcoin -- and arguably bullish for Bitcoin since it avoids adding BTC sell pressure.
insights.speaker: Peter Schiff (bear commentator)
insights.so_what: Investors should separate the MSTR-equity dilution risk from the Bitcoin-price implication -- share dilution to avoid BTC sales is bearish for MSTR holders but not necessarily for BTC.
insights.quote: By selling MSTR at a huge discount to Bitcoin value per share, you needlessly destroyed shareholder value just to avoid selling Bitcoin.

insights.claim: Tom Lee's Bitmine has accumulated 4.8% of total ETH supply (5.77 million ETH), reaching 96% of his publicly stated 5% target within about a year, including through this bear market, prompting the hosts to argue he should raise the target further rather than stop.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: Continued large-scale ETH accumulation by a visible institutional buyer is a persistent demand-side tailwind for ETH; watch whether Lee raises his target as a sentiment signal.
insights.quote: He now has 4.8% of all ETH supply. That's 5.77 million ETH supply.

themes.theme: Robin Hood Chain vs. Base: The L2 War

insights.claim: Robin Hood's L2 chain overtook Coinbase's Base in daily activity on July 10th, reaching 117 user-operations-per-second versus Base's 93, despite having launched only a few weeks earlier -- an ascent the hosts attribute largely to Robin Hood's existing retail-app distribution.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: Distribution from an established retail app can bootstrap L2 usage faster than pure technical or DeFi merit -- a template other brokerages/exchanges may copy.
insights.quote: Robin Hood chain usage flipped base on July 10th, so six days ago from the time of recording.

insights.claim: On Robin Hood chain, the top protocol by TVL is Morpho at $152 million (powering the in-app 7% USDG yield feature), followed by Athena at $110 million, Uniswap at $40 million and Maple Finance at $30 million, while the dominant activity by transaction volume is memecoin trading through competing token launchpads after early leader Noxa shut down amid internal team disputes.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: Real DeFi TVL is building but memecoin speculation remains the primary usage driver on the new chain, typical of early-stage L2 bootstrapping.

insights.claim: Base creator Jesse Pollak publicly admitted the social/creator-coin direction he pushed for Base through 2025 (the Farcaster/Zora-adjacent strategy) was the wrong bet, calling Q1 2026 a low point, and is stepping back from running the Base app while a colleague, Kobe, takes over app-layer strategy, pivoting toward DeFi and trading ('internet of finance') instead.
insights.speaker: Jesse Pollak (Base creator)
insights.so_what: Base's pivot toward trading/DeFi is a direct competitive response to Robin Hood chain's early success and signals where Coinbase will direct resources going forward.
insights.quote: The collateral damage talking about this year how things have gone has been an exercise in eating [ __ ].

insights.claim: Austin Campbell argued Base has been 'an outright failure' and a costly distraction that kept Coinbase from fixing its core business, a critique the hosts view as overstated since Base remains a top-tier L2 by usage (recently overtaken to roughly fourth place) and its underlying infrastructure still works even as the application-layer strategy resets.
insights.speaker: Austin Campbell
insights.so_what: Investors shouldn't read Base's app-layer stumble as terminal -- infrastructure value and chain ranking remain intact even amid a strategic pivot.

insights.claim: The hosts frame the real competitive battle as being between the Coinbase.com and Robin Hood apps for retail distribution, not the underlying chains -- Base was originally hyped for giving an L2 unprecedented centralized-exchange distribution (echoing Binance/BNB Chain), but Robin Hood replicated that advantage quickly while Coinbase lagged in adding tradfi (stock trading) features that Robin Hood already offered.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: L2 competitive advantage increasingly hinges on parent-app distribution and cross-asset feature velocity, not just technical differentiation.

insights.claim: Robin Hood chain's leading memecoin Cash Cat (roughly $104 million fully diluted valuation) and a comparably sized Solana memecoin from Anom (roughly $190 million FDV) launched around the same time near the market bottom, and the hosts are using their relative performance as a dual barometer of risk appetite and which ecosystem is winning the memecoin battle.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: Relative performance of Cash Cat versus Anom's coin can be watched as a real-time gauge of speculative risk appetite and Robin Hood-vs-Solana memecoin dominance.

themes.theme: ETH Tokenomics: Store of Value vs. Revenue Debate

insights.claim: An Ark Invest analyst's breakdown of Robin Hood chain's revenue split shows Robin Hood keeps roughly 90% margin ($816,000 in revenue), Arbitrum as tech provider takes about 10% ($80,000), and Ethereum L1 has captured only $1,538 (about 0.15%) in blob-space settlement fees since inception.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo (Ark Invest analyst, cited)
insights.so_what: This is the core bear case for ETH as a revenue-generating asset: L2 growth does not proportionally translate into L1 fee capture, pressuring fundamentals-based ETH valuation.
insights.quote: Robin Hood gets 90% margins, arbitum gets 10% and Ethereum gets 0.15% for data availability.

insights.claim: The same analyst argued a 'healthier split' would have Ethereum L1 capture at least 15% of L2-generated margin rather than roughly 0.15%, framing the current arrangement as ETH being underpaid for the settlement security it provides to L2s like Robin Hood chain.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo (Ark Invest analyst, cited)
insights.so_what: If a rent renegotiation between L2s and Ethereum L1 gains traction, it could materially shift ETH's fee-revenue economics -- a catalyst worth monitoring.
insights.quote: He said a healthier split would be at least Ethereum captures 15% of this margin.

insights.claim: Longtime Ethereum L2 bull Plenny returned after a long silence to admit his 2021-2023 expectations for application demand growth were wrong, arguing blockspace supply exploded while demand for applications proved highly inelastic and grew negligibly, undermining the original fee-driven ETH-as-money thesis.
insights.speaker: Plenny / Plenia (L2 commentator, cited)
insights.so_what: A prominent L2 bull's public reassessment signals the fee-driven ETH valuation thesis has structurally weakened, reinforcing a pivot toward store-of-value framing.
insights.quote: My expectations for growth in application demand from 2021 and 2023 post have proven to be wrong.

insights.claim: Plenny frames Ethereum's choice as binary: either subsidize fees and compete for the multi-trillion-dollar store-of-value market (accepting near-zero L2 fee revenue), or shrink blockspace capacity to hike transaction fees and compete in a shrinking billion-dollar fee market, which he pegs as consistent with roughly a $100 ETH endgame.
insights.speaker: Plenny / Plenia (L2 commentator, cited)
insights.so_what: This framing crystallizes the core debate investors must resolve before valuing ETH: fee maximization implies a far lower terminal price than a pure store-of-value framing.
insights.quote: ETH is money implies a market cap far higher than revenue could support.

insights.claim: David argued Ethereum no longer needs to prioritize L2 fee revenue -- after ten years of 'bootstrapping,' ETH should optimize purely as a store-of-value and scaling asset, similar to how Bitcoin and gold don't optimize for fee revenue, making cheap blockspace and scaling the priority over fee capture.
insights.speaker: David
insights.so_what: If this view gains consensus among Ethereum's core community, expect continued deprioritization of L1 fee revenue in protocol roadmap decisions, reinforcing a low-fee, high-throughput scaling strategy.
insights.quote: We're 10 years into the project now. ETH can afford to not care about fee revenue.

insights.claim: David argued that mixing a 'store of value' narrative with a 'valued on fee revenue' narrative is self-defeating for Ethereum -- noting the market already prices a dollar of Ethereum fee revenue far higher than a dollar of Microsoft or Amazon revenue -- and that Ethereum should stop discussing fees entirely, mirroring how Bitcoin scrubbed non-monetary use cases like timestamping from its narrative.
insights.speaker: David
insights.so_what: A narrative shift away from 'fee revenue' toward pure store-of-value framing could itself be a re-rating catalyst for ETH's valuation multiple, independent of fundamentals changing.
insights.quote: Fees are not the point of ether.

insights.claim: Joseph Lubin has publicly argued Ethereum L1 fees should stay low to foster ecosystem growth and market-share capture, with ETH's value ultimately accruing through its store-of-value status rather than fee revenue.
insights.speaker: Joseph Lubin (Ethereum co-founder, cited)
insights.so_what: A prominent Ethereum figure publicly backing the low-fee, store-of-value camp adds weight to that side of the ongoing tokenomics debate.
insights.quote: Ethereum L1 revenue fees should stay low to foster growth.

insights.claim: David estimated more than 50% probability that ETH's roughly 8-9% weekly rally versus Bitcoin's 2.5% (pushing the ETH/BTC ratio up 16% since June) reflects positive 'ETH is money' sentiment from Robin Hood chain's success rather than any actual L1 fee capture, while noting Tom Lee's continued buying is a confounding factor and one week of data is noisy.
insights.speaker: David
insights.so_what: If the ETH-is-money narrative genuinely drives price independent of fee capture, it validates the store-of-value re-rating thesis, but more weeks of data are needed to confirm the signal isn't noise.

insights.claim: Arbitrum founder Steven Goldfeder proposed that Ethereum should 'adopt' its largest rollups (Arbitrum, Base, Robin Hood chain) by treating a critical bug in any of them as an Ethereum-level vulnerability that would trigger an L1 fork, with those L2s paying Ethereum a much higher rent (roughly 10-20% versus today's ~0.15%) and dropping their independent security councils in exchange.
insights.speaker: Steven Goldfeder (Arbitrum founder)
insights.so_what: If adopted, this could materially increase Ethereum L1's fee capture from major L2s -- a structural catalyst worth tracking for ETH's revenue-based valuation case.
insights.quote: Ethereum should adopt its largest roll-ups in the sense that a critical bug in arbitum base or Robin Hood chain should be treated as an Ethereum vulnerability and trigger an L1 fork just like an L1 bug would.

insights.claim: David countered that Goldfeder's proposal risks overloading Ethereum's consensus and dependency surface, echoing Vitalik Buterin's earlier warning not to overload Ethereum consensus, and noted a simpler competing path already exists in native rollups, which inherently fork with L1 and start with no separate state or pre-existing distribution.
insights.speaker: David
insights.so_what: Native rollups offer an alternative way to tighten L2-L1 alignment without the governance entanglement Goldfeder's proposal would introduce.

insights.claim: The hosts frame Goldfeder's proposal as effectively monetizing Ethereum's unique decentralized-governance and coordination capability, which no other blockchain ecosystem currently offers, while questioning whether Ethereum's core client teams would actually want to take on governance responsibility over L2 state changes just to increase ETH monetization.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: Whether Ethereum's client teams are willing to expand consensus scope for monetization purposes is a key governance risk to watch before this proposal could advance.

insights.claim: The hosts estimated that if Ethereum L1 had captured a much larger share of Robin Hood chain's revenue (e.g., $100,000 instead of $1,538 in the first week), it would be bullish for ETH price in the near term, though David argued it would ultimately cap ETH's long-term valuation at a few hundred billion dollars rather than supporting a multi-trillion-dollar store-of-value valuation.
insights.speaker: David
insights.so_what: Investors betting on higher L2 rent capture for ETH should weigh the near-term price boost against a potentially lower long-run valuation ceiling if ETH becomes fee-revenue-priced.
insights.quote: bearish as hell. You're talking about a $100 ETH price. Fees don't matter.

themes.theme: Ethereum Foundation Spinoffs and DeFi Risk

insights.claim: The Ethereum Foundation's institutional privacy task force has spun off into ETH Systems, a for-profit venture financed by the same ETH treasury companies (Bitmine, Sharplink, Joseph Lubin) that fund earlier spinoffs ETH Labs and Ethereum Institutional, continuing a pattern of EF-adjacent entities being separately capitalized.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: The proliferation of EF-adjacent for-profit spinoffs funded by ETH treasury companies signals where institutional Ethereum capital and talent are concentrating.

insights.claim: A five-year Ethereum Foundation researcher, Francesco, departed for ETH Labs, which the hosts describe as becoming a landing spot for EF talent whose mission no longer fits inside the Foundation -- a trend they view as healthy for the broader ecosystem.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: The emergence of alternative outlets for EF talent could shape where core protocol development and research momentum shifts over time.

insights.claim: Despite broad crypto criticism that 'DAT' (digital asset treasury) companies are poor innovation, David argued they deserve credit specifically for financing alternative EF-adjacent entities ('Alt EFs'), calling that outcome phenomenal for Ethereum.
insights.speaker: David
insights.so_what: Even skeptics of the DAT trend broadly should note their capital has meaningfully funded Ethereum's institutional research and development ecosystem.
insights.quote: A lot of people are talking a lot of [ __ ] about DATs, Ryan. Like DATs are just terrible. Terrible innovation. Fake innovation. At least for one thing with Ethereum. They financed alt EFS which I think is phenomenal.

insights.claim: Contrary to the earlier 2026 narrative that AI-assisted hacking was driving a DeFi 'hack apocalypse,' data cited on the show shows April 2026 was the peak month for losses, and both the number and especially the dollar size of hacks have declined since, with attackers now picking off smaller 'abandonware' protocols as defenders also gain AI-driven hardening capability.
insights.speaker: Hib (crypto analyst, cited)
insights.so_what: The AI-hacking tail risk that had been weighing on DeFi/ETH sentiment appears overstated for now, removing one bearish overhang, though the risk could resurface with the next major AI capability jump.
insights.quote: the DeFi hack apocalypse as a false alarm.

insights.claim: A separate, underappreciated DeFi risk is 'vault permutation risk' -- yield vaults depositing into other vaults that deposit into further vaults, sometimes terminating in externally-owned accounts, creating opaque, layered counterparty chains behind seemingly simple yield products.
insights.speaker: Bankless host (David)
insights.so_what: Investors chasing vault yields should map underlying vault-of-vaults exposure rather than trusting the headline yield or protocol alone -- a growing, underpriced risk distinct from smart-contract exploits.

insights.claim: David argued the buy-and-burn token model is 2026's dominant winning design, citing Hyperliquid (HYPE), Venice (VVV), Lit Protocol, and Jito (JTO, via its new JTX trading interface that burns 80% of fees) as leading examples, arguing the model works because it requires genuine fee revenue and demonstrates fiduciary commitment to token holders.
insights.speaker: David
insights.so_what: Tokens with credible buy-and-burn mechanics tied to real revenue may command valuation premiums; watch for more projects adopting the model as a bullish signal for token-holder alignment.
insights.quote: The buy and burn token model is undefeated.

insights.claim: Crypto commentator Anom argued buybacks and burns alone don't explain valuation: Hyperliquid generates $800 million in annualized revenue and trades at a $65 billion valuation, while Pump.fun generates $440 million in annualized revenue (roughly half of Hyperliquid's) but trades at only $1.4 billion, attributing the gap to a 'trust premium' since Hyperliquid launched with no VC allocation while Pump.fun raised a $1 billion ICO and never delivered a promised airdrop.
insights.speaker: Anom (crypto commentator, cited)
insights.so_what: Token valuation premiums increasingly reflect a legitimacy/trust factor around team conduct and holder alignment, not just revenue or burn mechanics -- a qualitative factor investors should weight alongside financials.
insights.quote: Hyperlid makes 800 million in annualized revenue. Pump Fund makes 440 annualized revenue, but Hype trades at 65 billion, whereas Pump trades at 1.4 billion.

insights.claim: The hosts connect the trust-premium thesis to revenue durability: memecoin-launchpad trading fees (Pump.fun's core revenue) are viewed as less durable than perpetual-futures trading fees (Hyperliquid's core revenue), suggesting the market prices both business-model durability and social legitimacy when valuing buy-and-burn tokens.
insights.speaker: Bankless host
insights.so_what: Revenue quality and durability, not just headline revenue figures, should factor into how investors value fee-generating, buy-and-burn tokens.
